figures.caption: Table 5: DACS advantage as a function of decisions per agent D, holding N fixed. Accuracy delta and context efficiency ratio grow as D increases for the baseline; DACS accuracy is stable.
Table 6: All 8 scenarios across three phases. DACS accuracy is consistently high (90.0–98.4%); baseline collapses under agent count and decision density pressure.

Figure 3: Phase 3 (decision density scaling) results for s7 (N = 5, D = 8) and s8 (N = 3, D = 15). Error bars: ±1 SE (10 trials each). (a) Steering accuracy. (b) Wrong-agent contamination. (c) Average context tokens at steering time.
Table 2: Phase 1 results: steering accuracy, contamination, and context size across N ∈ {3, 5, 10}. Values are mean ± SE over 10 trials. ∆: DACS minus baseline. Ctx ratio: baseline mean / DACS mean.

Figure 1: DACS vs. flat-context baseline across N ∈ {3, 5, 10} (Phase 1). Error bars: ±1 SE (10 trials each). (a) Steering accuracy. (b) Wrong-agent contamination. (c) Average context tokens at steering time.
Table 1: Comparison of context management mechanisms.

Table 7: Phase 4 results: real-agent N -scaling validation. Agents are autonomous LLMs (Claude Haiku 4.5) generating free-form steering questions. Two independent judges shown. Phase 1 rows reproduce Table 2 values for cross-paradigm comparison. Welch’s t-test significance on per-trial Haiku-judge accuracy.

Figure 4: Phase 4 real-agent vs. Phase 1 synthetic results at matched N ∈ {3, 5}. (a) M1 steering accuracy (Haiku judge) for DACS and baseline across both paradigms. (b) M3 average context tokens at steering time. Error bars: ±1 SE (10 trials per condition). DACS advantage direction and growth with N are consistent across paradigms; absolute accuracy is lower under real agents due to the harder free-form question regime.
Table 3: Phase 2 results across homogeneous, crossfire, and cascade scenarios. Each cell: mean ± SE over 10 trials.

Figure 2: Phase 2 (agent diversity) results across s4 homogeneous, s5 crossfire, and s6 cascade. Error bars: ±1 SE (10 trials each). (a) Steering accuracy. (b) Wrong-agent contamination. (c) Average context tokens at steering time.
